title: 命案目睹记
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\136-命案目睹记-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Inspector Claddock discusses the medical history of Mr. Crickenshaw with Dr. Campbell. Claddock inquires whether the doctor suspects poisoning, noting that Crickenshaw had previously claimed the doctor suspected someone was poisoning him. Dr. Campbell admits that while he officially diagnosed Crickenshaw with severe gastroenteritis, the symptoms were similar to arsenic poisoning. However, the doctor notes that the symptoms only appeared when the whole family was home for dinner, whereas they did not occur when only the household staff were present. Dr. Campbell also mentions that a former partner, Mr. Morris, suggested his suspicions might be foolish because the symptoms did not align with regular, small doses of arsenic. Claddock considers the possibility that someone might benefit from the death of Luther Creech, but Dr. Campbell defends Emma, noting the symptoms only manifest during larger gatherings.

The conversation is interrupted by Alexander and Stoddard, two children who claim to have found a significant lead. To avoid being disturbed, they lead Claddock to a cluttered tack room near the stables. The boys explain that they searched bushes, hollow trees, and garbage cans before finding a dusty, crumpled envelope in a zinc tank in the boiler room, which Mr. Hillman had been using to burn scrap paper. Stoddard presents the envelope to Claddock. The envelope is addressed to Madeline Crickenshaw at an address in France. Alexander suggests this proves Edmund's French wife has been at the estate, while Stoddard speculates that she might actually be the person in the coffin.
